blocks.content: Neid immediately showed interest.
But their conversation was cut short when the bell rang.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: The teacher's coming. Let's go in for now.
Shirone set aside thoughts of the club and focused on class.
The day ended with the last lesson at 5 p.m.
Shirone packed his bag and left the classroom with Neid.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Let's talk to Iruki first.
Neid took Shirone to the club.
Upon arriving at the magical warehouse Estus, Shirone stared blankly at the building.
If the Estus he'd seen last time was a clustered colony, now it was a structure with multiple towering spires.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: This is really amazing every time I see it.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: There are 89 buildings, so the patterns are endless. I hope Iruki's awake.
The interior of Estus was as complex as its exterior.
But Neid navigated the maze-like paths effortlessly and headed for the research room.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I've been curious—how do you find your way? If the structure keeps changing, markings wouldn't help, right?

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Hehe. We don't use such low-level methods. We memorize everything. A senior from the club once restored Estus's blueprints.
Shirone was stunned.
It wasn't just about drawing the blueprint of a single building but understanding the mechanics of a massive, pattern-shifting complex.
Neid pointed to the ceiling of the hallway.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: See those three dots? That means this is Room 3. If it's Room 3, check the color of the iron door in the third warehouse. It's blue, right? So this place is called 'Room 3's Blue.'
Shirone's gaze followed Neid's finger.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Once you confirm that, count the number of warehouses connected to this one. There are four branching paths, so four warehouses. Now, here's the important part—convert the room number to X, the color to Y, and the number of warehouses to Z, then plug them into an equation.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What equation?

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: An equation the seniors created by analyzing Estus's patterns. Input the numbers, and you get a two-decimal solution. That number determines the direction. So if you memorize this equation, you'll never get lost in Estus. It's like a mathematical master key.
Shirone wondered if all this effort was necessary for club activities.
Estus, with its immense patterns, and the seniors who'd calculated them all to create a single equation.
It made him feel like there was something extraordinary about this club.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: But wouldn't it take too long to calculate every time the warehouse changes?

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Only at first. With practice, it becomes second nature. A routine. After about three months, you'll start to feel it intuitively.
Shirone nodded in understanding.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: That's incredible. An equation as the key to escaping a maze.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: One of the founding members must've had a Servant ability like Iruki. Even Iruki said the equation was breathtakingly beautiful when he first saw it.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Anyway, this must never be leaked. If the master key gets out, it'll be a disaster.
Shirone was grateful Neid had shared such a significant secret.
But it also felt like Neid was subtly encouraging him to join the club.
A place filled with the unproductive, obsessive passions of its founding members.
That was the Supernatural Parapsychology Club, hidden within the labyrinth of Estus.
After walking for about ten minutes, they reached the research room.
As before, the sign hung crookedly.
Opening the door unleashed a cloud of dust, but this time they covered their mouths in advance, avoiding disaster.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Iruki, we're here.
Neid spoke without looking, and Iruki, who'd been writing equations on the blackboard, turned around.
Seeing Shirone, he grinned.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Hey, Shirone. What brings you here? I thought you'd never return to this gloomy place.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Haha! Right. But now he wants to join.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Oh?
Iruki's eyes widened in surprise.
In his mind, a model student like Shirone didn't belong here.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Joining is one thing, but I also came for advice. I thought you guys might be able to help.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: The genius who defeated me has a problem. It must be something extraordinary.
Now familiar with Iruki's personality, Shirone wasn't offended by the sarcasm.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: After the Speed Gun test, something's felt off. I've been having nightmares, and when I wake up, I feel eerie. Like someone's beside me. Could this be related to the supernatural or parapsychology? You guys are experts in this stuff, right?
Neid and Iruki's eyes sparkled.
As explorers and critics of the unknown, they were fascinated by Shirone's words.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Then you've come to the right place. Here, take a seat.
Neid dusted off the sofa, sending a massive cloud into the air.
Shirone flailed his arms in panic.
He finally realized the absurd amount of dust was from Iruki's chalk powder.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: No, I'll just stand. Please, don't do anything in this room. Or at least clean up occasionally.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Now, now. Don't let a little dust bother you, oh first client of our club. State your business.
Neid and Iruki pulled up chairs and sat with their legs crossed, waiting like professionals.
Though their arrogance was grating, Shirone was desperate enough to speak.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Well… I've been having these dreams lately…
Shirone described the dreams he'd been having every night and the strange sensations that struck him at random.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I have no idea what's going on. Could a ghost have possessed me? Have you heard of anything like this before?
An unexpected silence fell.
Shirone gulped.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What? Why are you two like this? Are you messing with me again?

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Well… that does sound like something.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What do you mean? Explain it properly.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: That sensation you described—the eerie, unseen presence. In parapsychology, it's called 'transcendental awareness.'

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Transcendental awareness? Parapsychology?

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Two hundred years ago, an experiment was conducted on a death row inmate. It was called 'soul transmission'—the idea that if the soul was made of matter, it could be decomposed with electricity.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Here's how it worked: the inmate was sealed in a conductive metal box, and an electric current was passed through. But it was deemed too inhumane and stopped midway. The inmate survived but was unconscious for a month.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Here's the weird part: when he woke up, he knew everything that had happened outside during that time.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: That's impossible. How?

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Exactly—it's unbelievable. The researcher hypothesized 'soul ionization.' The soul was electrically decomposed, generating electromagnetic force that pulled information from the world to the inmate.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Of course, the academic world dismissed him as a madman. There were even rumors the inmate had bribed guards for information to save his life.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Wow. That kind of experiment actually happened.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Mainstream scholars don't acknowledge it, but it's still a hot topic in parapsychology.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Plus, the Church of Spirits, an official religious group, recognizes trance states. So soul ionization isn't entirely dismissed.

blocks.speaker: Neid
blocks.content: Anyway, that sensation the inmate felt is called 'transcendental awareness' in this field.
Shirone's spine tingled.
The term 'transcendental awareness' connected to his nightmares, making him wonder if it could be real.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Now it's time for my conclusion.
Shirone snapped out of his thoughts and looked up.
He was curious how the logical Iruki would interpret this.
In this field, the two made a surprisingly great team.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: But first, I have a question. This 'transcendental awareness' started after the Speed Gun test, right?

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Your photon output that day was definitely unusual. If it's a secret, you don't have to share, but it might help.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Shirone agreed.
To analyze the result, they needed the cause.
Besides, he hadn't planned to keep it a secret from his friends—he'd just been too flustered at the time.
Shirone detailed his journey through the infinite realm—the epiphany in the sequence of numbers and the transcendental awakening under the pressure of the duel.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: The Immortal Function.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Immortal Function?

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: I heard about it as a kid. The absolute function that can unravel all the world's secrets.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: But in my opinion, it's incredibly dangerous. 'All' is functionally the same as 'nothing.'
It aligned with what Etela had said, reminding Shirone just how risky his attempt had been.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I'd only thought about it—I never expected to actually reach that point. I never want to experience it again.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Quite the opposite. Like I said, the Immortal Function is already a studied mental state in academia.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: The mind isn't like knowledge. Just because you understand it intellectually doesn't mean anyone can do what you did.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Anyway, hearing your story makes me feel a bit better. Well, to defeat me, you'd have to reach the Immortal Function. Kekekeke.
Iruki's eccentricity, treating even Etela's warnings like a party trick, was unsettling.
But it also strangely reassured Shirone.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Hmm. So that was the Immortal Function. Got it. That helps a bit.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Really? You've figured it out?
Iruki turned his head and stared at the wall as if drawing equations on its blank surface.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: I'm not an expert in parapsychology, so I don't know much about 'transcendental awareness.'

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: But those nightmares you've been having—I have a rough idea what they are. And why you're having them.
Shirone clenched his fists unconsciously.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Tell me. Anything. I'm ready to hear even the most absurd theories.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: First, dreams distort or exaggerate memories. But your dreams are likely direct projections of memories.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: It's called psychological regression—like how hypnotized people recall their past with vivid clarity.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Assuming your nightmares are of that kind…

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Assuming they are…?

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: What you saw was probably… the universe.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: The universe?
Shirone knew what the universe was—the vast world beyond this one.
But his knowledge ended there.
Or rather, who could possibly know?
Since ancient times, humanity had known the universe existed, but no one had ever seen it.
The only beings to experience the universe were dragons.
Six hundred eighty years ago, Saint Dragon Gramish decided to leave the planet.
Before representatives of each race, he ascended toward the cosmos.
But after just thirteen hours, he crashed back to the ground.
His corpse was said to have been gruesome.
Following Gramish's final words, the representatives divided his remains and returned to their homelands.
The evidence gathered later played a significant role in advancing cosmological theories.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What I saw in my dreams… was the universe?
